perturbation.original_text: All actions by each Party related to this Agreement will comply with applicable laws and regulations. Notwithstanding anything in this Agreement to the contrary, no provision will be interpreted or applied so as to require a Party or its Affiliates, to do, or to refrain from doing, anything which would constitute a violation of, or be penalized by, any applicable laws and regulations or result in a loss of economic benefit under such laws or regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: All actions by each Party related to this Agreement will comply with its internal policies. Notwithstanding anything in this Agreement to the contrary, no provision will be interpreted or applied so as to require a Party or its Affiliates, to do, or to refrain from doing, anything which would result in a loss of economic benefit under its internal policies.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to comply with applicable laws and regulations introduces legal risk, as it suggests that parties can prioritize their internal policies even if they conflict with the law. This would make the contract non-compliant.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By removing the clause requiring compliance with applicable laws, the modified text suggests a willingness to prioritize internal policies over legal obligations. This creates a loophole that may violate the implied duty of good faith if a party acts contrary to established law while relying on the 'economic benefit' clause linked to its internal policies.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement to comply with applicable laws and regulations, replacing it with compliance to internal policies. This contradicts the principle of good faith required in contractual performance, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: 'Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By prioritizing internal policies over legal obligations, the changed text creates a loophole that could lead to actions not in good faith, especially if internal policies conflict with the law. This aligns with the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction,' because the contract omits a legally mandated adherence to laws, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The validity and interpretation of this Agreement and the legal relations of the Parties to it will be governed by the laws of the State of New York without recourse to its conflicts of law rules.
perturbation.changed_text: The legal relations of the Parties to this agreement will be governed solely by the decisions of the Steering Committee as outlined in Article 3.
perturbation.explanation: This omission removes the governing law provision, which is legally problematic. The contract now lacks a jurisdictional framework and is governed entirely by the Steering Committee. This introduces a legal contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Lack of choice of law provision and enforceability of contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law allows parties to specify New York law as the governing law under certain conditions. Removing this clause creates uncertainty, as it neglects to specify a legal system for resolving disputes. The steering committee may not be sufficient to replace the importance of state law in resolving disputes.
perturbation.location: ARTICLE 13 - ARBITRATION AND GOVERNING LAW, Paragraph 13.01
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The original contract text specifies that New York law governs the agreement. The changed text removes this provision and instead states that the legal relations of the parties will be governed solely by the decisions of the Steering Committee. This omission creates a legal contradiction because it eliminates the established legal framework for interpreting and enforcing the contract. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is incomplete, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both emphasize the importance of specifying a legal system (like New York law) for resolving disputes, which the changed text fails to do. The original text provided legal certainty while the changed text replaces it with a non-legal, internal governance mechanism, which would likely be insufficient and unenforceable in a court of law.

perturbation.original_text: In the event the Parties are unable to agree upon a resolution within a reasonable period of time, not to exceed sixty (60) days after first notice of the difference unless otherwise agreed in writing, any dispute arising out of or relating to this Agreement may be referred to final and binding arbitration before three arbitrators under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce. Each Party will appoint one arbitrator within thirty (30) days of notice of such referral and the two (2) so appointed will, within thirty (30) days from the appointment of the last of the two (2) arbitrators, select a third arbitrator who will act as the Chairman. The arbitration will take place in New York City, New York and the proceedings will be conducted in the English language.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event the Parties are unable to agree upon a resolution within a reasonable period of time, any dispute arising out of or relating to this Agreement will be resolved at the sole discretion of ExxonMobil.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the arbitration clause and grants ExxonMobil sole discretion in resolving disputes. This creates a legal imbalance and is against fair practice.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under New York law (NY CLS UCC § 2-302), a court may refuse to enforce a contract or clause that is found to be unconscionable at the time it was made. By granting ExxonMobil sole discretion to resolve all disputes, the modified text creates a situation where the agreement is excessively one-sided and unfair to FuelCell Energy, thus raising concerns about unconscionability and potential unenforceability.
perturbation.location: ARTICLE 13 - ARBITRATION AND GOVERNING LAW, Paragraph 13.02
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-302 Unconscionable Contract or Clause Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 3 Section 2--302. Unconscionable Contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the arbitration clause and gives ExxonMobil sole discretion in resolving disputes. This contradicts NY CLS UCC § 2-302, as referenced in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that a court may refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable at the time it was made. Granting one party sole discretion to resolve all disputes makes the agreement one-sided and unfair, potentially leading to unconscionability, and thus creates a legal contradiction by removing a fair dispute resolution process.
